reviews.review_text:
Are you picking this up ? Good. This is a remaster of two classic RTS games from the 90s, some of the best.

This collection includes the original entry, later called Tiberium Dawn and Red Alert. It also includes all expansions: Covert Operations, Counterstrike and Aftermath, as well as the exclusive console missions. There are also a secret goofy campaign for each game that needs to be unlocked with a secret input code. Dinosaurs and giant ants, they have their own briefings and are included as well.

Original C&C started it all, at the time the RTS genre was basically Westwood previous game, Dune 2, and the new game from Blizzard called Warcraft. C&C started the trend of FMV cutscenes featuring real actors with CGI backgrounds. These are just great, tons of campyness and cheese. The main game mostly consist of the same formulae, build your base, gather resources and destroy your enemy. The plot starts when a new and dangerous mineral crashes into Earth and starts growing everywhere. The two main forces, the GDI and the Brotherhood of NOD, start war against each other for the control of tiberium and the world. NOD is directed by Kane, an enigmatic messianic and terrorist man who will appear in every game of the saga, including the first Red Alert in some kind of crossover.

Red Alert starts when Einstein timetravels to the past to make Hitler disappear, which starts a new sequence of events leading to the soviets amassing power and not being kept in bay anymore. The two factions in here are the Allied and the Soviets. Your soviet briefings are given directly by Stalin, a sort of General Melchett from Blackadder, mixed with strong russian accent. It's just amazing.

They also included new elements to keep the genre fresh. Some missions will have a timer to be completed adding a sense of urgency. There's also internal, no base building missions where you generally play as a super commando and a couple of soldiers trying to find something. These missions involve intense micromanaging and a puzzle component. They are some of the hardest missions in the game but are quite rewarding.

Red Alert also adds naval and more air units. The naval ones in particular are not that interesting, with ships just firing and sinking everything in 2 or 3 shots, but it gives a little more variety.

The music is one of the highlights, memorable songs that are a delight to listen to. Every song can be accessed in the jukebox and even build a playlist. All units are also voiced and will react to your commands.

This is one of those remasters where it's running on top of the original code. You have that fun feature of insta switching between old graphics and new. The team also released the source code with bugfixes and patches, a great asset for modders.

The remaster adds a ton of enhancements and features. Hovering over the building menues now explicity tells you if units are strong vs infantry, vehicles or buildings. there's a ton of behind the scenes extras, including recordings of the actors briefings with greenscreen, unreleased music tracks, photos, etc. All these extras unlock one by one by beating missions.

On the bad side, some of the FMVs look like a pixel mess. They lost the HD original recordings but it seems they focused more on upscaling the first FMVs of each game, and then just stopped caring for the others, specially the CGI ones.

Also, the first C&C can sometimes be unfair and unbalanced. GDI seems to be much stronger than Soviets, and they don't even have access to air units. Hard difficulty should only be reserved for achievement hunters. This is the only game where an engineer will instantly takeover any enemy building by entering it. Expect APC vehicles with 5 engineers inside to completely takeover a base in 10 seconds.

The AI is also not the best. Units will get stuck, will go to deadends instead of taking direct routes. Enemy AI cheats a lot too. They will instantly replace destroyed buildings in the same location, even if they are not connected to other buildings. Sometimes you have to exploit the engine to make it more balanced. An infamous strategy is to put sandbags everywhere to block the enemy and avoid them rebuilding, for some reason they won't destroy the walls. Fortunately most of this was addressed in Red Alert.

On Red Alert side, there's a specific mission which is splitted into 2 (10A and 10B). They share a timer to finish them all, but the game won't tell you this. Expect to replay the mission from scratch if you start the second one with little time. They also neglect to tell you that there's a critical unit hidden in the edge of the map. It was like that in the original releases, but it would have been nice to add a little more briefing in this case.

C&C was huge, probably peaking with Red Alert 2. This is the best way to play the first entries again. Totally recommended.

I was squealing like a schoolgirl who had just met her idol when I first saw the announcement message from Joseph "Kane" Kucan himself on Youtube.
(EA doing fanservice with no strings attached? Has hell frozen over??)

This duo of games are basically the grandfather, the Doom of RTS genre, starring banger soundtrack from the equally legendary Frank Klepacki, and the gameplay is just like I remember it, from back when I was in high-school.

Anyway, if you're a fan of RTS games, can 100% recommend this.
This game is really Wonderful & such a great classic in it's remastered to play for the 1st time and it's my 1st time diving into the C&C remastered collection!!!

A classic, the grandfather of RTS. 

Still in its old pixelly goodness!

One of the games of my childhood, and one of the very first RTS games I ever played.

For years, I had been wondering whether Command & Conquer was truly a great RTS, or merely a great early RTS — and yes, there is a difference. Does it still stand the test of time, or was it mostly a generational thing for millennials who happened to meet it at the right moment?

Almost thirty years later, I came back looking for answers. Here are my honest thoughts.

Sure, with hindsight, many things could have been done better. Some campaign missions could have been a little less “hardcore” — by which I mean infuriating, unforgiving, and sometimes demanding pixel-perfect micromanagement from the player. Some gameplay mechanics also feel arguably redundant today. I’m looking at you, silos and minimap-as-a-building. Infantry tends to die in the blink of an eye, and, even worse, your own units are sometimes so good at friendly fire that four flamethrower soldiers can easily turn each other in torches simply because they are standing in the wrong positions.

And yet, we can only say this with the benefit of hindsight. For 1995, this was a massive leap forward compared to Westwood’s previous RTS, Dune II. And despite all the rough edges mentioned above, the game is still very playable — not least thanks to the excellent Remaster.

If you commit to completing the whole campaign, you get something more than just an old strategy game. You get a surprisingly well-balanced mix of 90s Desert Storm-inspired military paranoia, grim sci-fi, subtle humor — see the Moebius sequences — and glorious B-movie energy. Even though much of the game is ultimately about building a huge horde of tanks and steamrolling the enemy, it constantly reminds you that war is fought on many levels: military, media, cyber, political, and even spiritual.

So no, this is definitely not just a millennial nostalgia phenomenon. This is the beginning of a lineage that lasted for more than a decade — and sadly ended with C&C4 and browser games.

A rough game in places, absolutely. But also a genuinely important and still impressive one.
Great retro RTS. Hopefully they remaster red alert 2 and generals zero hour

Hard as Hell, but excellent. Recommended to experienced RTS players who want to try the first two games that truly perfected the formula, and are confident in their skills. This remaster seamlessly polishes up these two classics, removing the handful of quality of life flaws the originals had.

Quick tip: Kill the enemy harvesters trucks in C&C1. The AI gets nearly 30 times your income per trip, and *will* win a war of attrition.
The best remaster of the best in the series. Lots of ove went into these cult classic tyrants.
Some missions are a blast! Some are so annoying! But the fmv videos are still so memorable that this gets an overall recommendation for me.

Good remaster of a classic game. Definitely worth it when on sale.
Highly recommended if you like RTS games.

Being open-source and freeware, the best way to play C&C 1 on modern systems is the Nyerguds distribution and the best way to play Red Alert 1 is the FunkyFresh distribution, both of which are available via CnCNet for free and include a functional multiplayer client with plenty of options. This is the same game, but with a modern interface and many fixed bugs and other minor alterations, which means it tests different skills. It also keeps track of which missions you've completed and on which difficulty you did so. And it's got multiplayer replays, after a fashion. I think they work by introducing an automated remote host that remembers the random seed, although this does occasionally introduce a spot of lag. 

The Remastered Tiberian Dawn's Hard Mode is a brand new mode backported from RA1, and used to be even harder, but the steam community got it dialed back. Modern balance is a beefier war factory and a slightly later engi-apc rush. When the remaster released, Turkey had a faction bonus but it got removed because it was too good (virtually none of the bonuses worked in vanilla RA1).

This is not the preferred way to play the old games, but it's easier on the eyes and it works through Steam on your monitor's native resolution, and for some people that's enough to make it the preferred version.
Really fun, used to play it a long long time ago and now with it being online i get to play it with friends.

For King and Country!

Look, this is for nostalgia and nothing else. If you didn't play this when you were young, well, it might not be for you (but give it a shot anyway). If you did play this when you were young, you are going to LOVE it. Such a great remaster and so much fun!

just as fun and awesome as I remember playing on the PlayStation. The updated videos are actually really nice to look at, and so are the regular game graphics. Well worth the buy! Specifically given today's modern world news. Really fun to play as the Allies and crush those weak Soviets; even in-game, they are a paper tiger.

Cant believe that EA did something good. I grew up original red alert 1 and i have to say they did great, it almost feels like the old one , They did great and has all expansions including the playstation ones, They remastered the mussic. Please EA give redalert 2 and tiberian sun the same love
Booting up Command & Conquer Remastered feels like opening a time capsule from 1995 — except this time the graphics don’t look like someone smeared Vaseline on your screen and called it “fog of war.”
